This is a much cleaner way of doing things, and I think for small data sets, like the single scalar `dropout_rate`, it's likely fine.

However, one reason I moved away from `feed_dict` when going to the multi-gpu code is that  `feed_dict` is known to be slow[[1](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/issues/2919)]. In the thread [nanddalal](https://github.com/nanddalal) found using `feed_dict` with a GPU was about 4-5 times as slow as not using it on a GPU.

Quoting [yaroslavvb](https://github.com/yaroslavvb) (who was part of Google Brain and is part of OpenAI) from the thread referenced above:

> Feed_dict does a single-threaded memcpy of contents from Python runtime into TensorFlow runtime. If data is needed on GPU, then you'll have an additional CPU->GPU transfer. I'm used to seeing up to 10x improvement in performance when switching from feed_dict to native TensorFlow (Variable/Queue)

If we use `feed_dict`, we need to be careful not to put more stuff into `feed_dict`. (Which I guess is easiest if it's not there :-) )

You can make the call on keeping `feed_dict` or not. But just tell me your decision so I can start integrating my pull request in to yours before we merge yours.

I'm not sure if it's a good idea to have the right click to freeze option for lasso method.

In polygonal mode, it is necessary to freeze the mask for an object before moving on to draw around another object to generate its mask, and that is why the right click approach exists, so the user can tell the program that they are finished with one object and want to move on the next one. 

But for lasso, the selection of vertices are different that of the polygonal mode, as the user has to draw around an object continuously by pressing and holding the left button, and the mouse release in itself determines that the user has finished marking the object.

A lot of geocoding services provide a rich set of parameters, options and API methods. But more than 28 parameters indeed look rather unusual though.

Generally implementing full support for all parameters and methods is not a goal in geopy. Implementing basic forward/reverse geocoding is usually enough for a lot of use-cases. For missing features [the doc suggests][semver] to open PRs for adding those (yup, the wording might have been more explicit with regards to missing API parameters).

While it's possible to explicitly define all of these peculiar parameters, a really small subset of users would really need them. And even then they would probably be better off using a specialized SDK package for that geocoder, which goal is exactly to match the API as close as possible. But, having all these rarely needed parameters implemented in geopy, would force us to rename the geocoder class once they change their API version (like it was done with `Google` and `GoogleV3` a while ago), if they drop or change some of the parameters; in order to not silently break the code of people who did use these parameters. But the large group of people, who didn't, would have to rename the class to upgrade, while other changes from them are not needed.

So the general approach is trying to keep the geocoder's interface sufficient enough for typical use-cases. Easier API version upgrades is more of a consequence of that rather than a goal.

The exact set of parameters is up to you. For me what's already there looks fine.

[semver]: https://geopy.readthedocs.io/en/1.14.0/#semver

I see a few of these `np.dot` calls.  I always found it clearer to write `A.dot(B)` rather than `np.dot(A,B)`.  Infix rather than prefix is I guess closer to the infix math notation `A * B`.  If you prefer to leave it this way, it's fine, but I just thought I'd put that out there.

I find the code easier to read with assert, but the error message when there's a problem is a bit more informative / easier to read with np.testing (just in my opinion).  For example, consider

```
x = 1
y = 3
assert x==y,'badness happened'
```
which gives the following:
```
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
/Users/rmandelb/<ipython-input-6-1ea81ded12f9> in <module>()
----> 1 assert x==y,'badness happened'

AssertionError: badness happened
```

This tells me the code that failed.  But if I had done
```
np.testing.assert_equal(x,y,err_msg='badness happened')
```
I would have gotten
AssertionError: 
```
Items are not equal: badness happened
 ACTUAL: 1
 DESIRED: 3
```
which tells me how wrong things were, instead of just that they were wrong.

I could make this change, but it still leaves open the possibility that an issue could arise from a convolution of three or more profiles. Eg., convolving:

A - Not real-space analytic
B - Hard edges, real-space analytic
C - Hard edges, real-space analytic

This isn't likely to arise (it would require eg. a PSF with hard edges), but it's possible. In fact, I think I did something almost like this myself once for other work, except A was real-space analytic so problems didn't arise. But if it hadn't have been, a warning would have been raised no matter what I tried to do. So, I think it is proper to put this check here.

@tseaver Using `HasField` is "necessary" to actually tell if a meaning is set or not. For example:

```python
>>> from gcloud.datastore import _entity_pb2
>>> v = _entity_pb2.Value()
>>> v._fields
{}
>>> v.HasField('meaning')
False
>>> v.meaning
0
>>> v._fields
{}
>>> v.HasField('meaning')
False
>>> v.meaning = 0
>>> v._fields
{<google.protobuf.descriptor.FieldDescriptor object at 0x7f54f824b050>: 0}
>>> v.HasField('meaning')
True
>>> v.meaning
0
```

we should add a function that wraps this to_app(q->get_pattern(i))->get_arg(0) and checks that indeed there is exactly one argument and that the function is "pattern". Since patterns are in the ast.h, the function would be a matcher on patterns. ast_manager has a method called "bool is_pattern(expr* e)". Patterns can be multi-patterns, so take more than 1 argument. The relevant matcher could then be bool is_pattern(expr* e, ptr_vector<expr>& args);.
You currently assume single patterns. Multi-patterns require matches on all argument terms (the set of patterns associated with a quantifier, on the other hand, are disjunctive: a match succeeds if one of the alternative patterns is matched).

Why do you use `set +e; set -e` here? The `cut` should always succeed, and the pipelines will absorb the status of `curl` (and `head`):
```shell
$ (set -e; curl -Is http://localhost:1234/this_url_does_not_exist; printf 'status: %s\n' $?); printf 'outer status: %s\n' $?
outer status: 1
$ (set -e; curl -Is http://localhost:1234/this_url_does_not_exist | head -n 1; printf 'status: %s\n' $?); printf 'outer status: %s\n' $?
status: 0
outer status: 0
```

The APIs that call non-constant smart contract functions need synchronization, this is required to avoid sending transactions for the same effect.

A user might make two or more HTTP requests with the same token and partner, since only first can succeed the additional ones will only burn gas.

I'd suggest changing this test to not use Ray tune (since the implementation of Ray tune may change). Instead, I'd consider doing something like the following

```python
driver1 = """
...
def serializer(obj):
    return 1

def deserializer(serialized_obj):
    assert serialized_obj == 1
    return 1

class Foo(object):
    pass

ray.register_custom_serializer(serializer=serializer, deserializer=deserializer)

@ray.remote
def f(x):
    assert x == 1
    return Foo()

for _ in range(10):
    time.sleep(0.1)
    assert ray.get([f.remote() for _ in range(10)]) == 1
"""
```

In `driver2` all of the `1`s should be replaced by `2`.

I'd make `num_cpus=1` so that all tasks run on the same worker.

Let's consider reserving the use of `>>>`. It is the Python interpreter prompt and the standard way of running [doc tests](https://docs.python.org/2/library/doctest.html) so people may think they should be able to cut and paste directly into a prompt to test something. By not using this prompt here we avoid possible confusion and could eventually support doc tests.
